ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Chilli Paneer as a prepared Indo-Chinese dish. Closest match is the cottage cheese (paneer) food product class; the stir-fried preparation and sauce components are not captured by this node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible anchor for a paneer-based cooked dish. No specific Indo-Chinese paneer preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chilli Paneer or Indo-Chinese paneer preparations.

allergens: gluten (soy sauce, cornstarch batter may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Chilli Paneer is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing roughly 650 kcal, 28 g protein, 45 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chilli Paneer exists in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for paneer (cottage cheese), stir-fried vegetables (bell peppers, onions), and standard Indo-Chinese sauce components (soy sauce, chili sauce, vinegar). Energy and macros are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for paneer-equivalent (cottage cheese, full-fat) and vegetable components. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation with deep-fried paneer cubes and wok-tossed sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Paneer in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated by analogy: paneer (cottage cheese) has a very low GI (~30) due to high protein and fat content; cornstarch batter and soy/chili sauces contribute moderate-GI carbohydrates. The blended dish GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low), consistent with mixed dishes dominated by high-protein dairy and moderate sauce carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (40 × 11.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 14.0; per 100 g GL = (40 × 11.67) / 100 = 4.67.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (400 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce and chili sauce; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Chilli Paneer to small portions and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce preparations to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~40) moderated by high paneer protein and fat, but added sugars in chili and soy sauces and cornstarch batter contribute to glycemic load; a 300 g serving provides approximately 35 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Chilli Paneer in moderate portions (150–200 g), pairing it with non-starchy vegetables and monitoring post-meal blood glucose, while avoiding versions with heavy sweet-chili sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat content (6.67 g saturated fat per 100 g); combined with high sodium (400 mg/100 g) and trans fat (0.17 g/100 g), this dish presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Chilli Paneer consumption, prefer air-fried or shallow-fried versions, and choose preparations with reduced oil and low-sodium soy sauce to mitigate saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bell peppers and onions provide antioxidants (vitamin C 20 mg/100 g); however, nightshades and dairy (paneer) may trigger inflammatory responses in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their personal response to dairy and nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, green chilies) and reduce or eliminate Chilli Paneer if symptoms worsen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Paneer is a widely popular vegetarian Indo-Chinese dish originating in India, representing the fusion of Indian dairy traditions with Chinese stir-fry cooking techniques. The dish features cubes of paneer (Indian cottage cheese) that are typically coated in a seasoned cornstarch batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then tossed in a wok over high heat with a bold, savory-spicy sauce. The sauce is built from soy sauce, chili sauce, rice vinegar, freshly minced ginger and garlic, and sliced green chilies, creating a complex umami-forward flavor profile with heat and slight tanginess. Crunchy bell peppers (capsicum) and onions are stir-fried alongside the paneer, adding texture, color, and micronutrient value. The dish is served in two main styles: dry (as an appetizer or snack) and with gravy (as a side dish accompanying fried rice or noodles). Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 217 kcal, 9.3 g protein, 15 g fat (6.7 g saturated), and 11.7 g carbohydrate, with notable calcium (117 mg) from paneer and vitamin C (20 mg) from bell peppers. Sodium is high at 400 mg per 100 g due to soy and chili sauces, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), moderated by the high protein and fat content of paneer. Chilli Paneer is a staple at Indian restaurants, street food stalls, and home kitchens across India, and is commonly served at lunch, dinner, and as an evening snack or party appetizer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chilli Paneer?
Is Chilli Paneer good for diabetics?

What is the GI of Chilli Paneer?
Chilli Paneer vs Paneer Tikka nutrition
meal_context: paired with fried rice or Hakka noodles

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chilli Paneer exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining approved-source data for paneer, stir-fried vegetables, and Indo-Chinese sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy are well-established, and the preparation method is well-documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chilli Paneer as a prepared dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g (0.17 g/100 g) is carried from the input record; this reflects residual trans fats from deep-frying oil and is plausible for a deep-fried preparation. 4. Cholesterol (26.67 mg/100 g) and saturated_fat_g (6.67 g/100 g) values from the input were originally attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden source); these have been retained as plausible estimates consistent with ICMR-NIN paneer data and are documented here as estimated rather than source-verified. 5. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (40 × 11.67 × 3) / 100 = 14.0 g; per 100 g GL = 4.67. 6. Portions in the input contained multiple duplicates; the canonical serving size of 300 g (1 plate) has been adopted as the default based on the most frequent value. 7. No Chinese, Malay, or Tamil names are applicable as this is an Indian (not Singaporean) dish; Hindi name चिल्ली पनीर is included as the primary local-language alias.
